content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Tom Lutzenberg 
PO Box 2544 
Sacramento, CA 95812 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-131 
Dear Mr. Lutzenberg: 
August 19, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does Sacramento Municipal Utilities District's ("SMUD' s") award of a $1,500 scholarship 
towards your tuition, fees, and required books and equipment at a higher education institution for 
the 2019-20 academic year constitute an award received in a manner not related to an official's 
status in a bona fide contest pursuant to Regulation 18942(a)(14)? 
. CONCLUSION 
Yes. As such, the award is not subject to the Act's gift limits, but it is reportable income on 
your statement of economic interests. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the Assistant Deputy Director over Fiscal Implementation with the California 
Department of Forestry & Fire Protection ("CalFire")- a position designated in the agency's 
conflict of interest code. You regularly make decisions regarding financial system implementation 
and are a designated approver of state financial payments and commitments in the system if needed. 
You have been notified that the Sacramento Municipal Utilities District ("SMUD") selected 
you as a recipient of its 2019 Educational Powering Future Scholarship. This scholarship is offered 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-131 
Page No. 2 
publicly to eligible SMUD members with SMUD utility accounts who have a requisite grade point 
average, are enrolled in a higher education institution, and studying eligible topics. The scholarship 
recipients are selected by an independent selection committee based upon financial need, 
community involvement, essay content, and academic achievements and record. SMUD will select 
as many as 21 scholarship recipients from the Sacramento area, and scholarships range from $1,500 
to $5,000. 
As a selected winner, SMUD intends to give you a $1,500 scholarship for the 2019-20 
academic year. The award is to be made out to the college institution where you are enrolled and to 
be applied to your tuition, fees, and required books and equipment. 
ANALYSIS 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as: 
[A]ny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in 
the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of 
business to members of the public without regard to official status. 
To reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by 
local public officials in three ways: 
• 
Gift Limit of $500: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain 
public officials. (Section 89503.) The current limit is $500 from a single source in a 
calendar year. (Regulation 18940.2.) 
• 
Gift Reporting: In order that the public is made aware of any potential influences 
from gifts, the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring 
that any gift of $50 or more ( or gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same 
source) received during the calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statement of 
economic interests. (Section 87207.) The term "income" includes any gift. (Section 
82030.) 
• 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from 
making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence the 
outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an 
aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official 
within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 
87103(e).) 
However, under some circumstances, a payment is not considered a gift under the Act's gift 
exceptions in Regulation 18942, even though the payment falls within the definition of a gift. In this 
regard, Regulation 18942(a)(14) provides that payments for the following are neither gifts nor 
mcome: 


File No. A-19-131 
Page No. 3 
(14) A prize or award received in a manner not related to the official's status in a bona fide 
contest, competition, or game of chance. A prize or award that is not reported as a gift shall be 
reported as income unless the prize or award is received as a winning from the California State 
Lottery. 
Accordingly, a scholarship that a public official receives in a bona fide competition, rather 
than due to his or her official status, is neither a gift nor income, although it must be reported by the 
official as income for purposes of disclosure on the official's statement of economic interest (Form 
700). However, the scholarship must be the same for the public official as would be offered any 
others. One of the main factors in making this determination is the nature of the pool of contestants 
in the competition and the specific circumstances of the award. For instance, we advised that a 
"Woman of the Year" award provided to a state assemblymember qualified as a bona fide 
competition unrelated to her official status since an independent panel of judges selected her from a 
large pool of contestants representing the fields of medicine, sports, social work, journalism and 
public service. (Pritchard Advice Letter, No. A-95-094; see also Maitland Advice Letter, A-99-158 
[ official was advised that a fellowship offered to her as a mid-career professional in a public policy 
field was an award received in a bona fide competition].) 
In this case, the scholarship is offered publicly to eligible SMUD members with SMUD 
utility accounts' who have a requisite grade point average, are enrolled in a higher education 
institution, and studying eligible topics. As many as 21 recipients will be selected in the Sacramento 
area by an independent selection committee, which will consider financial need, community 
involvement, essay content, and academic achievements and record. Based upon these facts, the 
eligibility criteria used by SMUD to determine the scholarship recipient is broad enough to include 
a large pool of contestants, and the facts provided indicate that you received the award due to your 
status as a public official, but rather you earned the scholarship based upon merit. Under the facts 
presented, the scholarship is not considered to be a gift under the Act. 
However, we again caution the scholarship must be reported as income on the official's 
statement of economic interest (Form 700). Moreover, to the extent that SMUD may be implicated 
by any decision before you as a CalFire employee, you have a potential interest in SMUD as a 
source of income and may wish to seek further advice prior to taking part in the decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
-·~ o~/, 
Ryan O'Connor 
0 
Counsel, Legal Division
